workAreas.title: Frontend
workAreas.description: Interfaces, design systems, browser work, and product polish.

workAreas.title: Backend
workAreas.description: Systems, data, security, and runtime operations.

workAreas.title: Mobile
workAreas.description: Swift, SwiftUI, iOS, and Apple-platform development, with room for future React Native branches.

workAreas.title: Workflow
workAreas.description: Files, docs, planning, release work, and research-to-output flows.

workAreas.title: Agent Engineering
workAreas.description: MCP, skill-building, prompting discipline, and LLM application work.

workAreas.title: Marketing
workAreas.description: Brand, strategy, copy, distribution, creative, SEO, conversion, and growth work.

collections.title: My Picks
collections.description: A short starter stack. These are the skills I reach for first.

collections.title: Build Apps
collections.description: Frontend, UI, and design work for shipping polished apps.

collections.title: Swift Agent Skills
collections.description: The main Swift and Apple-platform set in this library. Install it all at once or pick from it.

collections.title: Build Systems
collections.description: Backend, architecture, MCP, and security work.

collections.title: Test & Debug
collections.description: QA, debugging, CI cleanup, and observability.

collections.title: Docs & Research
collections.description: Docs, files, research, and writing work.

collections.title: mktg Marketing Pack
collections.description: The full upstream marketing-cli playbook. Install the whole set at once or pick from it.

skills.name: frontend-design
skills.description: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use for building web components, pages, dashboards, HTML/CSS layouts, or styling any web UI.
skills.author: anthropics
skills.whyHere: Still one of the stronger frontend skills around for interface craft, visual direction, and polished product work.

skills.name: pdf
skills.description: Use when the task involves reading, extracting from, merging, splitting, or generating PDF files.
skills.author: anthropics
skills.whyHere: Worth keeping as a stable copy for PDF-heavy work.

skills.name: xlsx
skills.description: Use when the task involves spreadsheets, XLSX files, tabular cleanup, or exporting structured data.
skills.author: anthropics
skills.whyHere: Worth keeping as a stable copy for spreadsheet work.

skills.name: docx
skills.description: Use when the task involves Word documents, DOCX files, templated reports, or document extraction.
skills.author: anthropics
skills.whyHere: Worth keeping as a stable copy for document work.

skills.name: pptx
skills.description: Use when the task involves PowerPoint files, slide decks, or generating presentation output.
skills.author: anthropics
skills.whyHere: Worth keeping as a stable copy for presentation work.

skills.name: mcp-builder
skills.description: Use when building, debugging, or shipping MCP servers, tools, and local integrations.
skills.author: anthropics
skills.whyHere: One of the clearest MCP skills in the ecosystem. Worth keeping close as a stable copy.

skills.name: skill-creator
skills.description: Use when creating, updating, or reviewing SKILL.md-based agent skills.
skills.author: anthropics
skills.whyHere: Useful when a repo needs a new skill or a cleanup pass on an old one.

skills.name: doc-coauthoring
skills.description: Use when drafting, revising, or coauthoring long-form docs, specs, or written deliverables with the user.
skills.author: anthropics
skills.whyHere: Still one of the better starting points for collaborative writing and document work.

skills.name: canvas-design
skills.description: Create beautiful visual art in .png and .pdf documents using design philosophy. Use for creating posters, art, designs, or other static visuals.
skills.author: anthropics
skills.whyHere: Useful for visual direction and design craft when a task starts rough.

skills.name: webapp-testing
skills.description: Use when QAing a web app, checking regressions, or turning manual test flows into concrete repros.
skills.author: anthropics
skills.whyHere: Good web QA guidance with real product-testing value.

skills.name: brand-guidelines
skills.description: Applies official brand colors, typography, and styling to artifacts. Use for creating branded content, marketing materials, or maintaining design consistency.
skills.author: anthropics
skills.whyHere: Useful when product work spills into brand and visual system decisions.

skills.name: backend-development
skills.description: Backend API design, database architecture, microservices patterns, and test-driven development. Use for designing APIs, database schemas, or backend systems.
skills.author: wshobson
skills.whyHere: Covers API design patterns, service architecture, and error handling at the backend layer. Fills a gap that frontend-focused skill sets leave open.

skills.name: database-design
skills.description: Database schema design, optimization, and migration patterns for PostgreSQL, MySQL, and NoSQL databases. Use for designing schemas or optimizing queries.
skills.author: wshobson
skills.whyHere: Gives agents real schema design guidance: normalization, indexing strategy, migration patterns. Most general skills treat the database as an afterthought.

skills.name: llm-application-dev
skills.description: Building applications with Large Language Models - prompt engineering, RAG patterns, and LLM integration. Use for AI-powered features or chatbots.
skills.author: wshobson
skills.whyHere: Covers prompt engineering, token management, and LLM integration patterns from a backend perspective. Relevant whenever the codebase talks to a language model.

skills.name: code-documentation
skills.description: Writing effective code documentation - API docs, README files, inline comments, and technical guides. Use for documenting codebases or APIs.
skills.author: wshobson
skills.whyHere: Still a good documentation skill, even after upstream reorganized around plugins.

skills.name: ask-questions-if-underspecified
skills.description: Clarify requirements before implementing. Ask 1-5 must-have questions to avoid wrong work. Use when requests are ambiguous, have multiple valid interpretations, or lack key details like scope, constraints, or acceptance criteria.
skills.author: thsottiaux
skills.whyHere: A clean guardrail against agents sprinting through half-specified work.

skills.name: best-practices
skills.description: Transform vague prompts into optimized Claude Code instructions. Adds verification, context, constraints, and proper phasing. Use when prompts lack test cases, specific locations, or success criteria.
skills.author: MoizIbnYousaf
skills.whyHere: Original library skill that sets the quality bar for prompts, constraints, and implementation workflow across the library.

skills.name: changelog-generator
skills.description: Use when turning commits, shipped work, or release notes into a readable changelog.
skills.author: composio
skills.whyHere: Practical release-note help for real ship cycles.

skills.name: content-research-writer
skills.description: Use when researching a topic and turning the findings into a usable written brief or draft.
skills.author: composio
skills.whyHere: Turns research into usable writing.

skills.name: openai-docs
skills.description: Use when the user asks how to build with OpenAI products or APIs and needs up-to-date official documentation, current model guidance, or GPT-5.4 upgrade help.
skills.author: openai
skills.whyHere: Gives the library a direct path into current OpenAI docs and model guidance.

skills.name: gh-fix-ci
skills.description: Use when GitHub Actions CI is failing and the task is to diagnose or fix the pipeline.
skills.author: openai
skills.whyHere: Worth keeping for CI cleanup and broken checks.

skills.name: figma
skills.description: Use the Figma MCP server to fetch design context, screenshots, variables, and assets from Figma, then translate nodes into production code.
skills.author: openai
skills.whyHere: A good fit for UI and design-engineering work.

skills.name: sentry
skills.description: Use when investigating production errors, tracing issues, or working from Sentry events.
skills.author: openai
skills.whyHere: Useful for debugging and observability work.

skills.name: playwright
skills.description: Use when browser automation, end-to-end testing, or reproducing UI behavior in a real browser is required.
skills.author: openai
skills.whyHere: Useful when a task needs real browser work, not guessed output.

skills.name: linear
skills.description: Manage issues, projects & team workflows in Linear. Use when the user wants to read, create or updates tickets in Linear.
skills.author: openai
skills.whyHere: I actually use Linear, so the workflow shelf should reflect that.

skills.name: figma-implement-design
skills.description: Translates Figma designs into production-ready application code with 1:1 visual fidelity. Use when implementing UI code from Figma files, when the user mentions "implement design", "generate code from Figma", "implement component", provides Figma URLs, or asks to build components matching Figma specs.
skills.author: openai
skills.whyHere: OpenAI's Figma-driven frontend implementation flow. Pairs with frontend-design — different taste, same shelf, designer-handoff path covered.

skills.name: shadcn
skills.description: Manages shadcn components and projects, including adding, searching, fixing, debugging, styling, and composing UI. Provides project context, component docs, and usage examples. Applies when working with shadcn/ui, component registries, presets, --preset codes, or any project with a components.json file. Also triggers for "shadcn init", "create an app with --preset", or "switch to --preset".
skills.author: shadcn-ui
skills.whyHere: shadcn/ui is common enough that the shelf should have a dedicated skill.

skills.name: emil-design-eng
skills.description: Use when the task needs sharper UI polish, motion judgment, spacing, or design-engineering craft.
skills.author: emilkowalski
skills.whyHere: Sharpens polish, spacing, motion, and restraint in a way nothing else here quite does.

skills.name: security-best-practices
skills.description: Perform language and framework specific security best-practice reviews and suggest improvements. Trigger only when the user explicitly requests security best practices guidance, a security review/report, or secure-by-default coding help. Trigger only for supported languages (python, javascript/typescript, go). Do not trigger for general code review, debugging, or non-security tasks.
skills.author: openai
skills.whyHere: Backend work needs a real security lens.

skills.name: notion-spec-to-implementation
skills.description: Turn Notion specs into implementation plans, tasks, and progress tracking; use when implementing PRDs/feature specs and creating Notion plans + tasks from them.
skills.author: openai
skills.whyHere: A useful bridge between a written spec and actual work.

skills.name: userinterface-wiki
skills.description: UI/UX best practices for web interfaces. Use when reviewing animations, CSS, audio, typography, UX patterns, prefetching, or icon implementations. Covers 11 categories from animation principles to typography. Outputs file:line findings.
skills.author: raphaelsalaja
skills.whyHere: High-signal UI/UX review skill for interface polish, implementation critique, and stronger frontend craft.

skills.name: swiftui-pro
skills.description: Comprehensively reviews SwiftUI code for best practices on modern APIs, maintainability, and performance. Use when reading, writing, or reviewing SwiftUI projects.
skills.author: twostraws
skills.whyHere: A solid anchor for day-to-day SwiftUI work.

skills.name: swiftui-ui-patterns
skills.description: Best practices and example-driven guidance for building SwiftUI views and components, including navigation hierarchies, custom view modifiers, and responsive layouts with stacks and grids. Use when creating or refactoring SwiftUI UI, designing tab architecture with TabView, composing screens with VStack/HStack, managing @State or @Binding, building declarative iOS interfaces, or needing component-specific patterns and examples.
skills.author: Dimillian
skills.whyHere: Good component and layout patterns for SwiftUI screens.

skills.name: swiftui-design-principles
skills.description: Design principles for building polished, native-feeling SwiftUI apps and widgets. Use this skill when creating or modifying SwiftUI views, iOS widgets (WidgetKit), or any native Apple UI. Ensures proper spacing, typography, colors, and widget implementations that look and feel like quality apps rather than AI-generated slop.
skills.author: arjitj2
skills.whyHere: Useful when the problem is taste, structure, or visual judgment.

skills.name: swiftui-view-refactor
skills.description: Refactor and review SwiftUI view files with strong defaults for small dedicated subviews, MV-over-MVVM data flow, stable view trees, explicit dependency injection, and correct Observation usage. Use when cleaning up a SwiftUI view, splitting long bodies, removing inline actions or side effects, reducing computed `some View` helpers, or standardizing `@Observable` and view model initialization patterns.
skills.author: Dimillian
skills.whyHere: Useful when a SwiftUI file has grown past the point of comfort.

skills.name: swiftdata-pro
skills.description: Writes, reviews, and improves SwiftData code using modern APIs and best practices. Use when reading, writing, or reviewing projects that use SwiftData.
skills.author: twostraws
skills.whyHere: Keeps SwiftData coverage current without freezing it in this repo.

skills.name: swiftdata-expert-skill
skills.description: Expert guidance for designing, implementing, migrating, and debugging SwiftData persistence in Swift and SwiftUI apps. Use when working with @Model schemas, @Relationship/@Attribute rules, Query or FetchDescriptor data access, ModelContainer/ModelContext configuration, CloudKit sync, SchemaMigrationPlan/history APIs, ModelActor concurrency isolation, or Core Data to SwiftData adoption/coexistence.
skills.author: vanab
skills.whyHere: A second strong SwiftData voice from upstream.

skills.name: swift-concurrency-pro
skills.description: Reviews Swift code for concurrency correctness, modern API usage, and common async/await pitfalls. Use when reading, writing, or reviewing Swift concurrency code.
skills.author: twostraws
skills.whyHere: Concurrency bugs are sharp in Swift. The shelf needs specialists here.

skills.name: swift-concurrency-expert
skills.description: Swift Concurrency review and remediation for Swift 6.2+. Use when asked to review Swift Concurrency usage, improve concurrency compliance, or fix Swift concurrency compiler errors in a feature or file. Concrete actions include adding Sendable conformance, applying @MainActor annotations, resolving actor isolation warnings, fixing data race diagnostics, and migrating completion handlers to async/await.
skills.author: Dimillian
skills.whyHere: Another strong pass on Swift concurrency, with a different angle than the others.

skills.name: swift-concurrency
skills.description: Diagnose data races, convert callback-based code to async/await, implement actor isolation patterns, resolve Sendable conformance issues, and guide Swift 6 migration. Use when developers mention: (1) Swift Concurrency, async/await, actors, or tasks, (2) "use Swift Concurrency" or "modern concurrency patterns", (3) migrating to Swift 6, (4) data races or thread safety issues, (5) refactoring closures to async/await, (6) @MainActor, Sendable, or actor isolation, (7) concurrent code architecture or performance optimization, (8) concurrency-related linter warnings (SwiftLint or similar; e.g. async_without_await, Sendable/actor isolation/MainActor lint).
skills.author: AvdLee
skills.whyHere: Worth keeping for AvdLee's take on Swift concurrency review and cleanup.

skills.name: swift-testing-pro
skills.description: Writes, reviews, and improves Swift Testing code using modern APIs and best practices. Use when reading, writing, or reviewing projects that use Swift Testing.
skills.author: twostraws
skills.whyHere: Swift testing deserves its own specialists.

skills.name: swift-testing
skills.description: Expert guidance on Swift Testing best practices, patterns, and implementation. Use when developers mention: (1) Swift Testing, @Test, #expect, #require, or @Suite, (2) "use Swift Testing" or "modern testing patterns", (3) test doubles, mocks, stubs, spies, or fixtures, (4) unit tests, integration tests, or snapshot tests, (5) migrating from XCTest to Swift Testing, (6) TDD, Arrange-Act-Assert, or F.I.R.S.T. principles, (7) parameterized tests or test organization.
skills.author: bocato
skills.whyHere: Useful when the work is test migration, setup, or everyday test cleanup.

skills.name: swift-testing-expert
skills.description: Expert guidance for Swift Testing: test structure, #expect/#require macros, traits and tags, parameterized tests, test plans, parallel execution, async waiting patterns, and XCTest migration. Use when writing new Swift tests, modernizing XCTest suites, debugging flaky tests, or improving test quality and maintainability in Apple-platform or Swift server projects.
skills.author: AvdLee
skills.whyHere: Adds a second strong testing voice for Swift codebases.

skills.name: swift-api-design-guidelines-skill
skills.description: Write, review, or improve Swift APIs using Swift API Design Guidelines for naming, argument labels, documentation comments, terminology, and general conventions. Use when designing new APIs, refactoring existing interfaces, or reviewing API clarity and fluency.
skills.author: Erikote04
skills.whyHere: API design leaks across a whole Swift codebase. This earns a place.

skills.name: ios-accessibility
skills.description: Expert guidance on iOS accessibility best practices, patterns, and implementation. Use when developers mention: (1) iOS accessibility, VoiceOver, Dynamic Type, or assistive technologies, (2) accessibility labels, traits, hints, or values, (3) automated accessibility testing, auditing, or manual testing, (4) Switch Control, Voice Control, or Full Keyboard Access, (5) inclusive design or accessibility culture, (6) making apps work for users with disabilities.
skills.author: dadederk
skills.whyHere: Mobile needed real accessibility coverage.

skills.name: swift-accessibility-skill
skills.description: Apply platform accessibility best practices to SwiftUI, UIKit, and AppKit code. Use it alongside any SwiftUI, UIKit, or AppKit skill. Use it whenever writing, editing, or reviewing platform UI, even when the user does not mention accessibility. Also use it when the user mentions VoiceOver, Voice Control, Dynamic Type, Reduce Motion, screen readers, a11y, WCAG, accessibility audits, accessibilityLabel, UIAccessibility, NSAccessibility, assistive technologies, or Switch Control. Not for server-side Swift, non-UI packages, or CLI tools.
skills.author: PasqualeVittoriosi
skills.whyHere: Keeps accessibility work visible when the code is Swift-first.

skills.name: appkit-accessibility-auditor
skills.description: Use when auditing a macOS AppKit interface for accessibility, especially VoiceOver, keyboard navigation, and semantics.
skills.author: rgmez
skills.whyHere: AppKit needs its own accessibility pass.

skills.name: swiftui-accessibility-auditor
skills.description: Use when auditing SwiftUI views for accessibility on iOS or macOS and you want concrete fixes.
skills.author: rgmez
skills.whyHere: SwiftUI needs its own accessibility pass.

skills.name: uikit-accessibility-auditor
skills.description: Use when auditing UIKit screens for accessibility issues, especially VoiceOver, Dynamic Type, and control semantics.
skills.author: rgmez
skills.whyHere: UIKit needs its own accessibility pass.

skills.name: swift-architecture-skill
skills.description: Use when choosing or refactoring Swift app architecture, including MVVM, TCA, Clean Architecture, and similar patterns.
skills.author: efremidze
skills.whyHere: Architecture choices compound fast in Swift codebases. A dedicated skill helps.

skills.name: core-data-expert
skills.description: Use when working with Core Data stack setup, fetches, concurrency, migrations, performance, or CloudKit sync.
skills.author: AvdLee
skills.whyHere: Core Data still shows up often enough to keep a specialist.

skills.name: swiftui-performance-audit
skills.description: Audit and improve SwiftUI runtime performance from code review and architecture. Use for requests to diagnose slow rendering, janky scrolling, high CPU/memory usage, excessive view updates, or layout thrash in SwiftUI apps, and to provide guidance for user-run Instruments profiling when code review alone is insufficient.
skills.author: Dimillian
skills.whyHere: SwiftUI performance needs its own audit skill.

skills.name: swift-security-expert
skills.description: Use when working with iOS/macOS Keychain Services (SecItem queries, kSecClass, OSStatus errors), biometric authentication (LAContext, Face ID, Touch ID), CryptoKit (AES-GCM, ChaChaPoly, ECDSA, ECDH, HPKE, ML-KEM), Secure Enclave, secure credential storage (OAuth tokens, API keys), certificate pinning (SecTrust, SPKI), keychain sharing across apps/extensions, migrating secrets from UserDefaults or plists, or OWASP MASVS/MASTG mobile compliance on Apple platforms.
skills.author: ivan-magda
skills.whyHere: The mobile shelf should have a Swift-specific security voice.

skills.name: ios-simulator-skill
skills.description: Use when testing or automating an iOS app through the simulator, including builds, UI navigation, accessibility checks, and simulator lifecycle tasks.
skills.author: conorluddy
skills.whyHere: Agents working on Swift apps need simulator workflows.

skills.name: writing-for-interfaces
skills.description: Use when someone asks to write, rewrite, review, or improve text that appears inside a product or interface. Examples: "review the UX copy", "is there a better way to phrase this", "rewrite this error message", "write copy for this screen/flow/page", reviewing button labels, improving CLI output messages, writing onboarding copy, settings descriptions, or confirmation dialogs. Trigger whenever the request involves wording shown to end users inside software, including apps, web, CLI, email notifications, modals, tooltips, empty states, or alerts. Also trigger for vague requests like "review the UX" when interface copy review is implied. Do not trigger for content marketing, blog posts, app store listings, API docs, brand guides, cover letters, or interview questions. This skill is for interface language.
skills.author: andrewgleave
skills.whyHere: Interface writing belongs next to UI work, not in a distant docs shelf.

skills.name: install-from-remote-library
skills.description: Use when installing skills from a shared ai-agent-skills library repo. Inspect the library first with `--list`, prefer `--collection` when one exists, and preview the plan with `--dry-run` before installing.
skills.author: MoizIbnYousaf
skills.whyHere: Encodes the remote-library guardrails so agents inspect first, prefer curated collections, and avoid surprise installs.

skills.name: curate-a-team-library
skills.description: Use when building a managed team skills library from scratch or refining one for a real stack. Map the user's work to shelves, browse before curating, write meaningful `whyHere` notes, and create a starter pack once the first pass is solid.
skills.author: MoizIbnYousaf
skills.whyHere: Captures the curator protocol in versioned form so agents build a usable library instead of a random pile of skills.

skills.name: share-a-library
skills.description: Use when a managed library is ready to publish to GitHub and hand to teammates as an install command. Run the Git and GitHub steps, then return the exact shareable `ai-agent-skills install` command.
skills.author: MoizIbnYousaf
skills.whyHere: Turns a finished local library into a real shared artifact with GitHub push steps and an installable handoff.

skills.name: browse-and-evaluate
skills.description: Use when exploring the ai-agent-skills catalog to find, compare, and evaluate skills before installing. Always use --fields to limit output size and --dry-run before committing to an install.
skills.author: MoizIbnYousaf
skills.whyHere: Teaches agents to browse and evaluate skills efficiently without flooding the context window.

skills.name: update-installed-skills
skills.description: Use when syncing or updating previously installed skills to their latest version. Always dry-run updates before applying, and check for breaking changes.
skills.author: MoizIbnYousaf
skills.whyHere: Encodes safe update patterns so agents never overwrite customizations without previewing first.

skills.name: build-workspace-docs
skills.description: Use when regenerating README.md and WORK_AREAS.md in a managed library workspace. Always dry-run first to preview changes.
skills.author: MoizIbnYousaf
skills.whyHere: Teaches agents to keep workspace docs in sync with the catalog using dry-run before writing.

skills.name: review-a-skill
skills.description: Use when evaluating whether a skill belongs in a library. Preview content, check frontmatter, validate structure, and decide whether to keep, curate, or remove.
skills.author: MoizIbnYousaf
skills.whyHere: Teaches agents a structured review protocol before skills enter or stay in a library.

skills.name: audit-library-health
skills.description: Use when checking the overall health of a skills library. Run doctor, validate, check for stale skills, and verify generated docs are in sync.
skills.author: MoizIbnYousaf
skills.whyHere: Encodes the full library health check workflow so agents catch drift before sharing.

skills.name: migrate-skills-between-libraries
skills.description: Use when moving skills between library workspaces or upgrading from a personal library to a team library. Export from one workspace, import into another.
skills.author: MoizIbnYousaf
skills.whyHere: Guides agents through safe cross-library migration without losing metadata or breaking dependencies.

skills.name: cmo
skills.description: A senior marketing operator for any project. Orchestrates 53 marketing skills to build brands, generate content, and distribute across channels. Use this skill whenever the user wants to do marketing — brand voice, copy, SEO, email, social, launches, or anything marketing-related. Also triggers on 'help me market', 'write copy', 'launch strategy', 'brand voice', 'SEO', 'content', 'email sequence', 'social posts', 'landing page', 'grow', 'audience', 'competitors', 'what should I do next for marketing', 'I need more users', 'how do I get people to care', or any marketing request. When in doubt about which marketing skill to use, start here — even if the user's request is vague or doesn't explicitly mention marketing.
skills.author: MoizIbnYousaf
skills.whyHere: Keeps cmo available from the upstream marketing-cli playbook without bundling a local copy into this library.

skills.name: brand-voice
skills.description: Define or extract a consistent brand voice that other skills can use. Three modes: Extract (analyze existing content), Build (interview-based), Auto-Scrape (from URL). Use when copy sounds generic, when starting any new project, when voice feels inconsistent across channels, when onboarding a new brand, or when any skill needs voice-profile.md but it doesn't exist yet. This is always the first skill to run for a new project. Make sure to use this skill whenever the user mentions tone of voice, brand personality, 'my copy all sounds the same', 'how should I sound', 'analyze my website voice', 'define my tone', or anything about making content sound more human or distinctive. Even if the user just says 'my marketing sounds generic' — that's a voice problem.
skills.author: MoizIbnYousaf
skills.whyHere: Keeps brand-voice available from the upstream marketing-cli playbook without bundling a local copy into this library.

skills.name: positioning-angles
skills.description: Find the angle that makes something sell. Use this skill whenever the user mentions positioning, angles, differentiation, unique selling proposition, 'how do I stand out', 'what makes us different', value proposition, messaging framework, or 'find the hook'. Also trigger when copy isn't converting (often a positioning problem), when marketing feels generic, when launching a product, creating a lead magnet, writing a landing page, or entering a crowded market. Even if the user is about to write copy without established positioning, run this first — the angle informs everything downstream. Generates 3-5 positioning angles with competitive web research and validation.
skills.author: MoizIbnYousaf
skills.whyHere: Keeps positioning-angles available from the upstream marketing-cli playbook without bundling a local copy into this library.

skills.name: audience-research
skills.description: Build detailed buyer personas and audience profiles from research. Use this skill whenever the user mentions audience, buyer persona, ideal customer, target market, ICP, watering holes, 'who am I selling to', customer research, or audience profile. Also use when content feels unfocused or generic (that's an audience problem), when conversion is low because messaging doesn't resonate, when starting any new project (audience should be first), or when any downstream skill needs audience.md but it doesn't exist yet. Even if the user doesn't explicitly ask for audience research, trigger this if they're writing copy or building a landing page without a clear audience defined. Three approaches: Quick Profile, Persona Build, Community Mining.
skills.author: MoizIbnYousaf
skills.whyHere: Keeps audience-research available from the upstream marketing-cli playbook without bundling a local copy into this library.

skills.name: competitive-intel
skills.description: Research and analyze competitors to find positioning gaps and strategic opportunities. Use this skill whenever the user mentions competitors, competitive analysis, competitor teardown, market landscape, 'who else does this', 'how are we different', or competitor research. Also trigger when the user is entering a new market, when positioning feels weak or generic, when preparing for a launch, when any downstream skill needs competitors.md but it doesn't exist, or when the user asks about differentiation or market gaps. Even if the user just names a competitor casually ('what does X do?'), this skill likely applies. Three modes: Quick Scan, Deep Teardown, Gap Finder.
skills.author: MoizIbnYousaf
skills.whyHere: Keeps competitive-intel available from the upstream marketing-cli playbook without bundling a local copy into this library.

skills.name: keyword-research
skills.description: Strategic keyword research powered by web search and brand context. Use this skill whenever the user mentions keywords, keyword research, SEO topics, content topics, 'what should I write about', content strategy, blog ideas, search traffic, or content planning. Also trigger when the user wants to plan what content to create, when existing content isn't attracting search traffic, when any skill needs keyword-plan.md but it doesn't exist, or when the user asks about SEO in the context of content creation. Even casual mentions like 'I need blog post ideas' or 'what topics should I cover' warrant this skill. 8-phase process from seed to content brief.
skills.author: MoizIbnYousaf
skills.whyHere: Keeps keyword-research available from the upstream marketing-cli playbook without bundling a local copy into this library.

skills.name: landscape-scan

Even if they just say "image" or "picture for this" — this is the skill.
